setup.name: Fibonacci Number
hints: Think about what smaller subproblem this reduces to — fib(n) depends on fib(n-1) and fib(n-2).
Once plain recursion works, notice you're recomputing the same values — that's your cue to memoize or go bottom-up.
setup.description: Return the nth Fibonacci value by defining the recurrence clearly and avoiding repeated recomputation.

setup.name: Reverse String
hints: Two pointers — one at each end, swap and move inward.
You're allowed to do this in-place; no extra array or built-in reverse needed.
setup.description: Reverse the character array in place by swapping from both ends until the pointers meet.

setup.name: Power of Two
hints: A power of two has exactly one bit set in binary — how would you check that with a bitwise trick?
n & (n-1) clears the lowest set bit; what does that give you for a power of two?
setup.description: Decide whether n is a power of two by using the binary property that only one bit should be set.

setup.name: Pow(x, n)
hints: Naive repeated multiplication is O(n) — can you halve the exponent each recursive call instead?
Handle negative n by inverting x and n; handle even/odd exponents differently in the recursion.
setup.description: Compute x raised to n efficiently with fast exponentiation instead of multiplying n times.

arrays.name: Two Sum
hints: Instead of checking every pair, what if you remembered numbers you've already seen?
For each number, check if (target - number) already exists in a hash map.
arrays.description: Find two indices whose values add to target by checking each number against a stored complement.

arrays.name: Contains Duplicate
hints: A set can tell you in O(1) whether you've seen a value before.
Walk the array once, adding to a set — if you ever see a value already there, you're done.
arrays.description: Return whether any value appears at least twice in the array.

arrays.name: Valid Anagram
hints: Two strings are anagrams if they have the same character counts.
Build a frequency map for each string and compare, or sort both and check equality.
arrays.description: Check whether two strings contain exactly the same character counts in a different order.

arrays.name: Ransom Note
hints: This is a character-count comparison, similar to anagrams.
Count letters available in magazine; for each letter needed, decrement and fail if it goes negative.
arrays.description: Determine whether the magazine contains enough copies of each character to build the ransom note.

arrays.name: Isomorphic Strings
hints: You need a consistent one-to-one mapping between characters of both strings.
Track mappings in both directions so no character maps to two different characters.
arrays.description: Verify that characters in one string map one-to-one and consistently onto the other string.

arrays.name: Group Anagrams
hints: Anagrams share the same sorted form or the same character-count signature.
Use the sorted string (or a count-based key) as the hash map key, and group originals under it.
arrays.description: Group words together when they share the same normalized anagram signature.

arrays.name: Longest Consecutive Sequence
hints: Sorting gives O(n log n) — can you do better with a set?
Only start counting a sequence from a number with no predecessor (num-1 not in the set) to keep total work O(n).
arrays.description: Find the length of the longest run of consecutive integers without sorting the whole array.

arrays.name: Range Sum Query - Immutable
hints: Repeated range sums are wasteful to recompute — precompute cumulative sums once.
prefix[i] = sum of first i elements; range sum(i,j) = prefix[j+1] - prefix[i].
arrays.description: Precompute prefix sums so any subarray sum can be answered in O(1).

arrays.name: Product of Array Except Self
hints: Without division, split the answer into 'everything left' and 'everything right' of each index.
One pass building left-products, one pass building right-products, then multiply them together.
arrays.description: Build the product for each index using left and right cumulative products without division.

arrays.name: Subarray Sum Equals K
hints: Prefix sums plus a hashmap of prefix-sum frequencies is the key combo here.
If prefixSum[j] - prefixSum[i] = k, a subarray sums to k — track how often each prefix sum occurs.
arrays.description: Count contiguous subarrays whose sum equals k by tracking how often each prefix sum has appeared.

arrays.name: Best Time to Buy and Sell Stock
hints: Track the minimum price seen so far as you scan left to right.
At each day, your best profit is today's price minus the lowest price seen before today.
arrays.description: Track the cheapest buy seen so far and the best profit obtainable after it.

arrays.name: Maximum Subarray
hints: Classic Kadane's — decide at each step whether to extend the previous subarray or start fresh.
If the running sum ever goes negative, reset it to the current element.
arrays.description: Find the contiguous subarray with the largest total sum.

arrays.name: Maximum Product Subarray
hints: A negative number can flip your minimum into your maximum — track both running max AND min.
Whenever the current number is negative, swap your running max and min before updating them.
arrays.description: Track both the current max and min products because a negative number can flip them.

arrays.name: Merge Sorted Array
hints: Merging from the front requires shifting elements — try merging from the back instead.
Compare the largest remaining elements of both arrays and place the bigger one at the end, working backwards.
arrays.description: Merge two sorted arrays into the first array in place by filling from the back.

arrays.name: Sort Colors
hints: This is the Dutch National Flag problem — can you sort with three pointers in one pass?
Keep low, mid, high pointers; swap 0s to front, 2s to back, leave 1s as mid advances.
arrays.description: Sort 0s, 1s, and 2s in one pass using the Dutch national flag partition.

arrays.name: Top K Frequent Elements
hints: Count frequencies first, then think about getting the k largest without a full sort.
A min-heap of size k, or bucket sort by frequency, both avoid an O(n log n) sort.
arrays.description: Return the k most frequent values without fully sorting all elements by frequency.

arrays.name: Continuous Subarray Sum
hints: Use prefix sums modulo k to detect when the same remainder repeats far enough apart.
If prefix[j] % k equals prefix[i] % k and j - i > 1, the subarray between them sums to a multiple of k.
arrays.description: Check whether a subarray of length at least two has a sum that is a multiple of k.

arrays.name: Maximum Sum Circular Subarray
hints: A circular max subarray is either the standard Kadane answer, or the total sum minus the minimum subarray.
Compute both the max subarray sum and the min subarray sum in one pass, then compare the two circular cases.
arrays.description: Compute the best non-circular subarray and compare it with the wraparound case.

arrays.name: Minimum Number of Arrows to Burst Balloons
hints: Sort intervals by end coordinate and greedily shoot an arrow at the end of the current overlap group.
Whenever the next interval starts after your current arrow position, you need a new arrow.
arrays.description: Shoot the fewest arrows needed so each interval balloon is hit at least once.

arrays.name: First Missing Positive
hints: The answer must be in [1..n+1], so values outside that range are noise.
Try putting value x at index x-1 until every useful value is either placed or impossible.
arrays.triggerQuestion: What phrase in the prompt reveals the intended pattern?
arrays.pitfall: Write the brute force first, then name the repeated work or invariant before coding.
arrays.description: Place positive values where they belong so you can identify the smallest missing positive in linear time.

arrays.name: Reverse Pairs
hints: Count cross pairs while merging sorted halves.
Do not reset the right pointer for every left value; monotonic movement is the trick.
arrays.triggerQuestion: What constraint makes the usual brute force too slow?
arrays.pitfall: Counting after sorting can destroy original order if you do it too early.
arrays.description: Count pairs where nums[i] is more than twice nums[j] while preserving near O(n log n) time.

arrays.name: Count of Smaller Numbers After Self
hints: Process from right to left with a Fenwick tree, or count during merge sort.
Coordinate compression is needed when values are large or negative.
arrays.triggerQuestion: What constraint makes the usual brute force too slow?
arrays.pitfall: Forgetting original indices loses where each count belongs.
arrays.description: For each element, count how many smaller values appear to its right.

arrays.name: Number of Ways to Split Array
arrays.description: Count split positions where the left-side sum is at least as large as the right-side sum.
hints: Precompute the total sum, then sweep once while maintaining the running left sum.
At each split position, compare leftSum with totalSum - leftSum and count valid cuts.

twoptr.name: Valid Palindrome
hints: Skip non-alphanumeric characters as you move two pointers inward from both ends.
Compare characters case-insensitively; stop when your pointers cross.
twoptr.description: Ignore non-alphanumeric characters and test whether the cleaned string reads the same forward and backward.

twoptr.name: Move Zeroes
hints: Use a slow pointer to track where the next non-zero element should go.
Fast pointer scans; whenever it finds a non-zero, swap it into the slow pointer's spot and advance both.
twoptr.description: Move all zero values to the end while keeping the relative order of non-zero values.

twoptr.name: Two Sum II - Input Array Is Sorted
hints: The array being sorted means you don't need a hash map — two pointers from both ends work directly.
If the sum is too big, move the right pointer left; if too small, move the left pointer right.
twoptr.description: Use the sorted order to find the target pair with two inward-moving pointers.

twoptr.name: 3Sum
hints: Sort the array first, then fix one number and two-pointer the rest.
For each index i, use two pointers on the remaining subarray to find pairs summing to -nums[i]; skip duplicates.
twoptr.description: Return all unique triplets whose values sum to zero without duplicate answers.

twoptr.name: 3Sum Closest
hints: Same sort-and-two-pointer skeleton as 3Sum, but track the closest sum instead of exact matches.
Compare abs(currentSum - target) against your best answer each time you move a pointer.
twoptr.description: Choose three numbers whose total is closest to the target value.

twoptr.name: Container With Most Water
hints: Start with pointers at both ends — moving the taller line inward can never help.
Area is limited by the shorter line, so always move the pointer at the shorter line inward.
twoptr.description: Maximize the water area formed by two vertical lines and the x-axis.

twoptr.name: Trapping Rain Water
hints: Water trapped at any position depends on the tallest wall to its left AND right — whichever is shorter.
Two pointers with running maxLeft/maxRight compute this in one pass without extra arrays.
twoptr.description: Compute how much water is trapped after raining by reasoning about left and right boundaries.

twoptr.name: Max Consecutive Ones III
hints: Think of this as a sliding window allowed up to k zeroes inside it.
Expand right; when zero-count exceeds k, shrink from the left until valid again.
twoptr.description: Find the longest window of 1s you can obtain after flipping at most k zeros.

twoptr.name: Longest Substring Without Repeating Characters
hints: Sliding window with a set (or map of last-seen index) tracks the current unique-character window.
When you hit a repeated character, shrink the window past its previous occurrence.
twoptr.description: Find the longest substring that contains no repeated character.

twoptr.name: Longest Repeating Character Replacement
hints: Track the count of the most frequent character in your current window.
Window is valid if (length - count of most frequent char) <= k; shrink from the left when violated.
twoptr.description: Find the longest substring that can be made uniform after replacing at most k characters.

twoptr.name: Permutation in String
hints: A fixed-length window compared by character-count matching tells you if a permutation exists.
Slide one character at a time, updating counts incrementally rather than recomputing from scratch.
twoptr.description: Check whether any substring of s2 is a permutation of s1.

twoptr.name: Fruit Into Baskets
hints: This is a sliding window that can contain at most 2 distinct fruit types.
Expand right freely; when a third distinct type appears, shrink from the left until only 2 remain.
twoptr.description: Find the longest subarray containing at most two distinct fruit types.

twoptr.name: Minimum Window Substring
hints: Track how many characters of t you still need inside your window using a count map.
Expand right until valid, then greedily shrink from the left to find the minimum window before expanding again.
twoptr.description: Return the shortest substring of s that contains every character required by t.

twoptr.name: Sliding Window Maximum
hints: A naive scan per window is O(nk) — a monotonic deque gets you O(n).
Keep indices in decreasing value order; pop from front when outside the window, pop from back when a new value makes older ones useless.
twoptr.description: Report the maximum value inside every contiguous window of size k.

twoptr.name: Shortest Unsorted Continuous Subarray
hints: Scan left-to-right tracking max to find the right boundary.
Scan right-to-left tracking min to find the left boundary.
twoptr.triggerQuestion: What constraint makes the usual brute force too slow?
twoptr.pitfall: Sorting a copy works, but misses the useful boundary invariant.
twoptr.description: Find the smallest subarray that must be sorted so the whole array becomes sorted.

twoptr.name: Minimum Swaps to Group All 1's Together
twoptr.description: Find the fewest swaps needed to gather all 1s into one contiguous block.
hints: The window size is fixed to the total number of 1s in the array.
Count how many 0s are inside each such window; that count is the swaps needed for that placement.

binsearch.name: Binary Search
hints: Keep two pointers, low and high, and narrow the range by comparing the middle element.
Watch your loop condition (<= vs <) and how you update low/high to avoid off-by-one errors.
binsearch.description: Locate the target in a sorted array by halving the search range each step.

binsearch.name: Search Insert Position
hints: This is binary search where you return the insertion index even when the target isn't found.
When low > high, low is exactly the correct insertion point — think through why.
binsearch.description: Return the index where target exists or should be inserted to keep the array sorted.

binsearch.name: Path With Minimum Effort
hints: Treat effort as the edge weight and minimize the maximum edge weight along the path.
Use a min-heap keyed by the current best effort so far; the path cost is the max edge seen, not the sum.
binsearch.description: Find a path from top-left to bottom-right that minimizes the maximum height difference on any step.

binsearch.name: Find First and Last Position of Element in Sorted Array
hints: Run binary search twice — once biased to find the leftmost occurrence, once for the rightmost.
When you find a match, don't stop — keep narrowing toward the boundary you're looking for.
binsearch.description: Use binary search to find the leftmost and rightmost occurrence of target.

binsearch.name: Search in Rotated Sorted Array
hints: At least one half (relative to mid) is always properly sorted — figure out which, then check if the target lies in it.
Compare nums[low] to nums[mid] to determine which side is sorted, then check if target falls in that range.
binsearch.description: Search a rotated sorted array by deciding which half remains ordered at each step.

binsearch.name: Find Minimum in Rotated Sorted Array
hints: The minimum is where the rotation 'breaks' — compare mid to the rightmost element to decide which half contains it.
If nums[mid] > nums[high], the minimum is to the right of mid; otherwise it's at or before mid.
binsearch.description: Identify the pivot point and return the minimum value in a rotated sorted array.

binsearch.name: Search a 2D Matrix
hints: Treat the 2D grid as a single sorted 1D array of length rows*cols and binary search it.
Convert your mid index back to (row, col) using division and modulo by the row width.
binsearch.description: Search a matrix that behaves like a flattened sorted array.

binsearch.name: Find Peak Element
hints: You don't need to scan the whole array — compare mid to its neighbor to pick a direction.
If nums[mid] < nums[mid+1], a peak exists to the right; otherwise it's at mid or to the left.
binsearch.description: Return any index whose value is greater than its neighbors.

binsearch.name: Koko Eating Bananas
hints: Binary search over the possible eating speeds, not over the array itself.
For a candidate speed, compute total hours needed; if within h hours, try a smaller speed, otherwise larger.
binsearch.description: Find the minimum eating speed that lets Koko finish all piles within h hours.

binsearch.name: Capacity To Ship Packages Within D Days
hints: Same shape as Koko — binary search over possible ship capacities.
For a candidate capacity, simulate greedily loading days; check if it fits within D days.
binsearch.description: Find the smallest ship capacity that can deliver all packages within the given days.

binsearch.name: Split Array Largest Sum
hints: Binary search over the answer (the largest subarray sum), checking feasibility with a greedy split.
Feasibility check: greedily add numbers until adding one more exceeds your candidate max, then start a new split.
binsearch.description: Split the array into k parts so the largest part sum is as small as possible.

binsearch.name: Median of Two Sorted Arrays
hints: Binary search the cut in the smaller array.
A valid partition has leftA <= rightB and leftB <= rightA.
binsearch.triggerQuestion: What phrase in the prompt reveals the intended pattern?
binsearch.pitfall: Write the brute force first, then name the repeated work or invariant before coding.
binsearch.description: Compute the median of two sorted arrays in logarithmic time by partitioning them correctly.

binsearch.name: Find Minimum in Rotated Sorted Array II
hints: When mid equals right, shrink right by one because the sorted side is ambiguous.
The duplicate case degrades worst-case complexity, and that is acceptable.
binsearch.triggerQuestion: What constraint makes the usual brute force too slow?
binsearch.pitfall: Using the no-duplicates template breaks on equal boundary values.
binsearch.description: Find the minimum in a rotated sorted array even when duplicates blur the pivot.

strings.name: Valid Palindrome II
hints: Two pointers from both ends; when characters mismatch, you get one chance to skip a character.
Write a helper checking palindrome-ness of a substring, and call it twice — skipping the left mismatch, then the right.
strings.description: Decide whether the string can become a palindrome after deleting at most one character.

strings.name: Longest Palindromic Substring
hints: Every palindrome has a center — try expanding outward from each possible center.
Handle both odd-length centers (single character) and even-length centers (between two characters).
strings.description: Return the longest contiguous substring that is itself a palindrome.

strings.name: Palindromic Substrings
hints: Same expand-around-center idea as Longest Palindromic Substring, but count every valid expansion.
Remember to check both odd and even center cases for every index.
strings.description: Count how many substrings of the string are palindromes.

strings.name: Minimum Number of Steps to Make Two Strings Anagram
hints: The answer is about the difference in character frequency counts between the two strings.
Count characters in both; sum up how many excess characters s has that t doesn't.
strings.description: Count the minimum character replacements needed to turn one string into an anagram of the other.

strings.name: Find All Anagrams in a String
hints: Sliding window of fixed length (p.length) with a running character-count comparison.
Update counts incrementally as the window moves, instead of recomputing from scratch.
strings.description: Return all start indices where a substring of s is an anagram of p.

strings.name: Find the Index of the First Occurrence in a String
hints: The brute-force check-every-position approach is O(n*m) and is often good enough — try that first.
For extra credit, look into the KMP algorithm, which avoids redundant comparisons using a prefix-function table.
strings.description: Return the first position where the pattern appears inside the text.

strings.name: Repeated Substring Pattern
hints: Think about what happens if you concatenate the string with itself and remove the first and last character.
If the original string appears anywhere inside (s+s) with the first/last char removed, it's built from repetition.
strings.description: Check whether the entire string is built by repeating one smaller substring.

strings.name: Longest Common Prefix
hints: Compare characters column by column across all strings, or repeatedly shrink a candidate prefix.
Start with the first string as your candidate, and trim it whenever it doesn't match the next string.
strings.description: Find the longest prefix shared by every string in the list.

strings.name: Word Pattern
hints: You need a consistent bijective mapping between pattern characters and words, like Isomorphic Strings.
Track mappings in both directions to catch cases where the mapping isn't one-to-one.
strings.description: Check whether the pattern letters and words form a one-to-one mapping.

strings.name: Longest Palindromic Subsequence
hints: This is a classic interval DP: compare the characters at both ends of the substring.
If the ends match, take 2 + dp on the inner substring; otherwise take the better of dropping either end.
strings.description: Find the longest subsequence, not necessarily contiguous, that reads the same both ways.

strings.name: Minimum Number of Steps to Make Two Strings Anagram
hints: The answer comes from comparing character counts between the two strings.
Count the frequency difference and total only the extra characters from the first string.
strings.description: Count the minimum character replacements needed to turn one string into an anagram of the other.

strings.name: Find All Anagrams in a String
hints: Use a fixed-size sliding window and compare character counts against the target string.
As the window slides, update the counts incrementally instead of recomputing them each time.
strings.description: Return all start indices where a substring of s is an anagram of p.

strings.name: Group Shifted Strings
hints: Strings belong together if the differences between adjacent letters match after wrapping around the alphabet.
Build a normalized key from the shift pattern, then group strings with the same key in a hash map.
strings.description: Group strings that become identical after shifting every letter by the same amount.

strings.name: Repeated DNA Sequences
hints: Every candidate has fixed length 10, so the window is simple.
A set for seen and a set for repeated is enough before optimizing to bit encoding.
strings.triggerQuestion: What phrase in the prompt reveals the intended pattern?
strings.pitfall: Write the brute force first, then name the repeated work or invariant before coding.
strings.description: Return the 10-letter DNA strings that appear more than once.

strings.name: Palindrome Pairs
hints: For each split, if one side is palindrome, look for the reverse of the other side.
The empty string and duplicate reverse cases need explicit care.
strings.triggerQuestion: What constraint makes the usual brute force too slow?
strings.pitfall: Most wrong answers miss empty string or self-pair filtering.
strings.description: Find index pairs whose concatenated strings form a palindrome.

linkedlist.name: Linked List Cycle
hints: Two pointers moving at different speeds will eventually meet if there's a cycle — Floyd's algorithm.
Move slow 1 step and fast 2 steps; if they ever point to the same node, there's a cycle.
linkedlist.description: Detect whether the linked list contains a cycle.

linkedlist.name: Middle of the Linked List
hints: Fast-slow pointers — when fast reaches the end, slow is at the middle.
Move fast two steps per one step of slow; handle even-length lists carefully.
linkedlist.description: Return the middle node of the linked list in one pass.

linkedlist.name: Linked List Cycle II
hints: Once slow and fast meet inside the cycle, a third pointer from the head finds the cycle's start.
After detection, reset one pointer to head and move both remaining pointers one step at a time — they meet at the start.
linkedlist.description: Find the node where a linked-list cycle begins.

linkedlist.name: Reorder List
hints: Break this into three sub-problems: find the middle, reverse the second half, then merge alternately.
Fast-slow finds the middle; in-place reversal handles the second half; then interleave node by node.
linkedlist.description: Rearrange the list into alternating front and back nodes without changing node values.

linkedlist.name: Reverse Linked List
hints: Track three pointers — previous, current, next — and flip the direction of each link as you go.
At each step: save next, point current.next to previous, then advance previous and current forward.
linkedlist.description: Reverse the entire singly linked list in place.

linkedlist.name: Swap Nodes in Pairs
hints: Think about it two nodes at a time — you need to relink four pointers per swap.
Use a dummy node before the head to simplify handling the very first swap.
linkedlist.description: Swap every adjacent pair of nodes in the list.

linkedlist.name: Reverse Linked List II
hints: This is a partial reversal — reverse only the sublist between positions left and right.
Use a dummy node, walk to just before 'left', then reverse that segment and reconnect it.
linkedlist.description: Reverse the list only between the given left and right positions.

linkedlist.name: Merge Two Sorted Lists
hints: This is like merging two sorted arrays, but relinking pointers instead of copying into a new array.
Use a dummy head node to simplify edge cases, always attaching the smaller of the two current nodes.
linkedlist.description: Merge two sorted linked lists into one sorted list.

linkedlist.name: Add Two Numbers
hints: This is digit-by-digit addition with carry, except digits are stored in reverse order as list nodes.
Track a carry variable across each step; don't forget a leftover carry after both lists are exhausted.
linkedlist.description: Add two numbers stored in reverse-order linked lists and return the sum as a linked list.

linkedlist.name: Merge k Sorted Lists
hints: Merging two lists at a time is easy — how do you scale that to k lists efficiently?
A min-heap holding the current head of each list lets you always pull the smallest node in O(log k).
linkedlist.description: Merge k sorted linked lists efficiently into one sorted list.

linkedlist.name: Remove Nth Node From End of List
hints: Two pointers with a fixed gap of n between them find the target node in a single pass.
Advance a lead pointer n steps ahead first, then move both together until lead reaches the end.
linkedlist.description: Delete the nth node from the end of the list in one traversal.

linkedlist.name: Copy List with Random Pointer
hints: A hash map from original node to its copy handles the random pointers cleanly in a second pass.
First pass: create copies and map original→copy. Second pass: wire up next and random using the map.
linkedlist.description: Clone a linked structure where each node has both next and random pointers.

linkedlist.name: LRU Cache
hints: You need O(1) get and put — a hashmap alone can't maintain order, so pair it with a doubly linked list.
Hashmap gives O(1) lookup; doubly linked list lets you move a node to the front and evict from the back in O(1).
linkedlist.description: Design a cache that supports O(1) get and put while evicting the least recently used entry.

linkedlist.name: Insert Delete GetRandom O(1)
hints: You need O(1) insert, delete, and random access — combine an array with a hash map.
Store value -> index in a map, and keep the values in an array so you can swap-delete in O(1).
linkedlist.description: Design a set that supports insert, delete, and random retrieval in average O(1) time.

linkedlist.name: Design Twitter
hints: This is a data-structure design problem: think in terms of maps, linked structures, and efficient feed retrieval.
You typically need user->follow set, user->tweets list, and a way to merge recent tweets efficiently when building the feed.
linkedlist.description: Design a miniature Twitter that supports posting, following, and fetching a merged news feed.

linkedlist.name: Merge Two Sorted Lists
hints: This is the same merge idea as merging sorted arrays, but you relink nodes instead of copying values.
Use a dummy head and always attach the smaller current node from the two lists.
linkedlist.description: Merge two sorted linked lists into one sorted list.

linkedlist.name: Sort List
hints: Linked-list merge sort is the standard approach here: split the list, sort each half, then merge.
Use fast-slow pointers to split the list in two, recursively sort both halves, and merge them back together.
linkedlist.description: Sort a linked list in O(n log n) time with constant or minimal extra space.

linkedlist.name: Reverse Nodes in k-Group
hints: Break the list into chunks of size k and reverse each chunk in-place, then reconnect them.
Before reversing a chunk, verify that you actually have k nodes available; otherwise leave the tail untouched.
linkedlist.description: Reverse nodes in chunks of size k and leave the final short group unchanged.

linkedlist.name: Design Tic-Tac-Toe
hints: This is a bookkeeping problem: you only need to track row, column, and diagonal counts for each move.
Each move updates a few counters; when any absolute count reaches n, that player wins.
linkedlist.description: Design a Tic-Tac-Toe game that reports the winner efficiently after each move.

stacks.name: Valid Parentheses
hints: A stack naturally matches the 'last opened, first closed' structure of valid brackets.
Push opening brackets; on a closing bracket, check if it matches the top of the stack, then pop.
stacks.description: Check whether every opening bracket is closed in the correct order.

stacks.name: Min Stack
hints: You need O(1) access to the minimum at any point, not just the current one.
Push (value, currentMin) pairs, or maintain a second stack tracking the running minimum.
stacks.description: Design a stack that can return the current minimum value in O(1).

stacks.name: Evaluate Reverse Polish Notation
hints: Postfix notation is a textbook stack application — numbers push, operators pop two and push the result.
On an operator, pop the top two values, apply it (mind order for subtraction/division), push the result.
stacks.description: Evaluate an arithmetic expression written in postfix notation.

stacks.name: Implement Queue using Stacks
hints: Two stacks can simulate FIFO order if you occasionally flip one's contents into the other.
Use one stack for enqueue; when dequeuing and the 'dequeue stack' is empty, pour the enqueue stack in, reversing order.
stacks.description: Implement FIFO queue operations using only stack behavior.

stacks.name: Design Circular Queue
hints: A fixed-size array with head/tail indices that wrap around using modulo gives O(1) operations.
Track current size (or use one extra empty slot) to distinguish a full queue from an empty one.
stacks.description: Design a fixed-size queue whose front and rear wrap around the array.

stacks.name: Next Greater Element I
hints: A monotonic decreasing stack finds, for each element, the next greater element to its right in one pass.
Pop elements smaller than the current one from the stack — they've found their answer.
stacks.description: For each number in nums1, find the next greater number that appears to its right in nums2.

stacks.name: Daily Temperatures
hints: Same monotonic stack idea, but track indices to compute the day-gap.
Keep a stack of indices with decreasing temperatures; on a warmer day, pop and compute the distance for each.
stacks.description: For each day, return how many days you must wait to see a warmer temperature.

stacks.name: Next Greater Element II
hints: The array is circular, so simulate wrapping by conceptually iterating through it twice.
Iterate indices 0 to 2n-1 using i % n, maintaining the same monotonic stack logic.
stacks.description: Find the next greater element for each array value when the array is treated as circular.

stacks.name: Car Fleet
hints: Sort cars by starting position; a car merges with the fleet ahead if its arrival time is ≤ the fleet's.
Process from the car closest to the destination backwards, using a stack to track fleet arrival times.
stacks.description: Count how many car fleets reach the destination after faster cars may catch slower ones.

stacks.name: Largest Rectangle in Histogram
hints: For each bar, you want to know how far it extends before hitting a shorter bar — a monotonic increasing stack finds this.
When a shorter bar appears, pop and calculate area using the popped bar's height and current index as the boundary.
stacks.description: Find the maximum rectangle area that can be formed under the histogram bars.

stacks.name: Min Remove to Make Valid Parentheses
hints: Track invalid parentheses indices with a stack or set, then rebuild the string without them.
First pass marks unmatched parentheses; second pass keeps only the valid characters.
stacks.description: Remove the fewest parentheses so the remaining string is valid.

stacks.name: Score of Parentheses
hints: Balanced parentheses can be scored with a stack or by tracking depth and contributions.
A simple approach is to push partial scores when you see '(', then combine them when you see ')'.
stacks.description: Compute the score of a balanced parentheses string using the given scoring rules.

stacks.name: Basic Calculator II
hints: Use a stack to handle operator precedence without building a full parser.
When you hit * or /, immediately combine with the previous number; + and - can be pushed as signed values.
stacks.description: Evaluate a string expression with +, -, *, and / while respecting operator precedence.

stacks.name: Basic Calculator
hints: This version adds parentheses, so you need to remember the current context while diving into a sub-expression.
A stack of signs / previous results lets you handle nested parentheses cleanly.
stacks.description: Evaluate an expression containing +, -, and parentheses.

stacks.name: Design a Stack With Increment Operation
hints: This is a stack implementation with one extra operation, so think carefully about what extra state you need.
A straightforward solution keeps the stack plus a lazy increment structure so increments do not need to touch every element.
stacks.description: Design a stack that also supports incrementing the bottom k elements.

stacks.name: Design Min Stack
hints: You need O(1) access to the minimum at any point, not just the current one.
Push value/min pairs, or maintain a second stack tracking the running minimum.
stacks.description: Design a stack API that supports push, pop, top, and retrieving the minimum in O(1).

stacks.name: Basic Calculator III
hints: This extends calculator parsing with parentheses and full operator precedence.
A stack-based parser can still work by folding numbers immediately for * and / while deferring + and - through signs or grouped results.
stacks.description: Evaluate an arithmetic expression containing parentheses and all four operators.

stacks.name: Remove Invalid Parentheses
hints: First figure out the minimum number of removals needed, then search for valid results with that removal budget.
A BFS or backtracking approach works well here; the key is to stop at the first layer that yields valid strings.
stacks.description: Remove the minimum number of parentheses and return all valid resulting strings.

stacks.name: Maximum Frequency Stack
hints: You need to pop the most frequent value, with recency as the tiebreaker.
Track frequency counts plus stacks grouped by frequency so push/pop stay O(1).
stacks.description: Design a stack-like structure that pops the most frequent element, breaking ties by recency.

stacks.name: Design Front Middle Back Queue
hints: This is a queue-structure design problem with operations at multiple positions, so think in terms of balanced halves.
Two deques or two balanced stacks can keep the front and back halves synchronized as elements are inserted and removed.
stacks.description: Design a queue that supports push and pop at the front, middle, and back.

stacks.name: Maximal Rectangle
hints: Treat every row as the base of a histogram of consecutive 1s.
Run largest-rectangle-in-histogram on each row.
stacks.triggerQuestion: What constraint makes the usual brute force too slow?
stacks.pitfall: The 2D problem becomes 1D repeatedly; do not invent a new rectangle algorithm.
stacks.description: Find the largest rectangle containing only 1s inside a binary matrix.

recursion.name: Subsets
hints: At each element, you have two choices — include it or don't — forming a recursion tree.
Backtrack: add the element to your path, recurse, then remove it before trying the next branch.
recursion.description: Subsets: identify constraints, choose an efficient strategy, and handle edge cases. Pattern focus: Subsets.

recursion.name: Implement Stack using Queues
hints: A queue can simulate a stack if you rotate elements after each push so the newest item reaches the front.
Use one queue and repeatedly move earlier elements behind the newly pushed one, or use two queues to perform the rotation.
recursion.description: Implement Stack using Queues: identify constraints, choose an efficient strategy, and handle edge cases. Pattern focus: Queue via two stacks.

recursion.name: Subsets II
hints: Same as Subsets, but with duplicates — skip duplicate choices at the same recursion depth.
Sort first, then skip an element if it equals the previous one and the previous wasn't used at this level.
recursion.description: Subsets II: identify constraints, choose an efficient strategy, and handle edge cases. Pattern focus: Subsets.

recursion.name: Combination Sum III
hints: This is combination backtracking with a fixed number of picks and a target sum.
At each step, choose the next number larger than the previous one to avoid duplicates and enforce ordering.
recursion.description: Combination Sum III: identify constraints, choose an efficient strategy, and handle edge cases. Pattern focus: Combinations.

recursion.name: Generalized Abbreviation
hints: Each character can be kept or abbreviated, so this is subset-style backtracking with a running count of skipped letters.
Accumulate consecutive skips into a number before appending the next literal character.
recursion.description: Generalized Abbreviation: identify constraints, choose an efficient strategy, and handle edge cases. Pattern focus: Subsets.

recursion.name: Gray Code
hints: You want a sequence where consecutive values differ by one bit, so build recursively or use reflection.
Generate n-1 bits first, then mirror the list with the high bit set to preserve the one-bit difference property.
recursion.description: Gray Code: identify constraints, choose an efficient strategy, and handle edge cases. Pattern focus: Permutations.

recursion.name: Permutations
hints: Track which elements are already used in the current permutation being built.
At each call, try every unused element next, mark it used, recurse, then unmark it (backtrack).
recursion.description: Permutations: identify constraints, choose an efficient strategy, and handle edge cases. Pattern focus: Permutations.

recursion.name: Increasing Subsequences
hints: This is subset-style backtracking with an extra increasing-order condition.
At each level, choose or skip the next number, but only extend the path if the new number keeps the sequence non-decreasing.
recursion.description: Increasing Subsequences: define recurrence transitions and reuse solved subproblems. Pattern focus: Subsets.

recursion.name: Permutations II
hints: Duplicates in the input need extra care to avoid generating the same permutation twice.
Sort first; skip a duplicate value if the identical previous value hasn't been used at this level.
recursion.description: Permutations II: identify constraints, choose an efficient strategy, and handle edge cases. Pattern focus: Permutations.

recursion.name: Combinations
hints: Similar to Subsets, but you fix the target size k and typically only move forward through the array.
Prune early: if remaining elements aren't enough to reach size k, stop that branch.
recursion.description: Combinations: compute the total number of valid outcomes under the given constraints. Pattern focus: Combinations.

recursion.name: Combination Sum
hints: Since numbers can be reused, including an element lets you pick it again in the next call.
Sort the array to prune — stop trying larger candidates once the running sum exceeds the target.
recursion.description: Combination Sum: identify constraints, choose an efficient strategy, and handle edge cases. Pattern focus: Combinations.

recursion.name: Combination Sum II
hints: Each number can be used once, and there are duplicates — apply Subsets II's pruning trick here too.
Sort first; at each level, skip an element if it's the same as the one you just skipped.
recursion.description: Combination Sum II: identify constraints, choose an efficient strategy, and handle edge cases. Pattern focus: Combinations.

recursion.name: Generate Parentheses
hints: Track the count of open and close brackets used so far as you build the string recursively.
You can add an open bracket if under n; you can only add a close if it wouldn't exceed the opens used.
recursion.description: Generate Parentheses: identify constraints, choose an efficient strategy, and handle edge cases. Pattern focus: N-Queens-style pruning.

recursion.name: Palindrome Partitioning
hints: At each step, try every possible 'cut' and only recurse into cuts that form a palindrome.
A helper checking if a substring is a palindrome, called at each candidate cut, keeps this clean.
recursion.description: Palindrome Partitioning: enforce character-level constraints with efficient matching and state tracking. Pattern focus: N-Queens-style pruning.

recursion.name: Word Search
hints: This is DFS with backtracking on a grid — try each direction, undo your visited-mark if that path fails.
Mark cells visited on the current path (mutate temporarily or use a set) and unmark when backtracking.
recursion.description: Word Search: identify constraints, choose an efficient strategy, and handle edge cases. Pattern focus: N-Queens-style pruning.

recursion.name: Letter Combinations of a Phone Number
hints: Each digit maps to letters; combinations form by choosing one letter per digit position, recursively.
Build the combination one digit at a time, recursing to the next digit and backtracking after each letter.
recursion.description: Letter Combinations of a Phone Number: compute the total number of valid outcomes under the given constraints. Pattern focus: N-Queens-style pruning.

recursion.name: N-Queens
hints: Place queens row by row, trying every column at each row, backtracking on conflicts.
Track used columns and both diagonal directions to check conflicts in O(1) instead of rescanning.
recursion.description: N-Queens: identify constraints, choose an efficient strategy, and handle edge cases. Pattern focus: N-Queens-style pruning.

recursion.name: Letter Case Permutation
hints: At each character, you either keep it as-is or toggle its case if it is a letter.
This is a permutation-style backtracking tree where each position branches into a small number of choices.
recursion.description: Letter Case Permutation: identify constraints, choose an efficient strategy, and handle edge cases. Pattern focus: Permutations.

recursion.name: Expression Add Operators
hints: Multiplication needs the previous operand so you can undo and reapply precedence.
Avoid numbers with leading zero unless the number itself is zero.
recursion.triggerQuestion: What phrase in the prompt reveals the intended pattern?
recursion.pitfall: Write the brute force first, then name the repeated work or invariant before coding.
recursion.description: Expression Add Operators: identify constraints, choose an efficient strategy, and handle edge cases. Pattern focus: Backtracking with Previous Operand.

recursion.name: Sudoku Solver
hints: Track used digits per row, column, and box for O(1) validity checks.
Choose the next empty cell with fewer candidates to reduce branching.
recursion.triggerQuestion: What constraint makes the usual brute force too slow?
recursion.pitfall: Plain brute force without constraint sets becomes painfully slow.
recursion.description: Sudoku Solver: identify constraints, choose an efficient strategy, and handle edge cases. Pattern focus: Backtracking with Constraints.

trees.name: Invert Binary Tree
hints: At each node, just swap its left and right children — then recurse into both.
This works bottom-up or top-down; either order of swapping and recursing gives the same result.
trees.description: Invert Binary Tree: traverse tree structure with correct state propagation and base cases. Pattern focus: DFS/BFS Traversal.

trees.name: Maximum Depth of Binary Tree
hints: The depth of a tree is 1 plus the max depth of its two subtrees.
A recursive base case (null returns 0) makes this a two-line function.
trees.description: Maximum Depth of Binary Tree: optimize for the best possible value while preserving problem constraints. Pattern focus: DFS/BFS Traversal.

trees.name: Same Tree
hints: Two trees are the same if their root values match AND both subtrees are also the same, recursively.
Handle the null cases carefully — both null is true, one null and one not is false.
trees.description: Same Tree: traverse tree structure with correct state propagation and base cases. Pattern focus: DFS/BFS Traversal.

trees.name: Subtree of Another Tree
hints: Check if the 'same tree' condition holds starting at every node of the main tree.
Reuse a same-tree helper, calling it once per node of the outer tree as you traverse it.
trees.description: Subtree of Another Tree: traverse tree structure with correct state propagation and base cases. Pattern focus: DFS/BFS Traversal.

trees.name: Diameter of Binary Tree
hints: The diameter through any node equals left-subtree height plus right-subtree height — track the max.
Compute height recursively, updating a running 'best diameter' variable as a side effect.
trees.description: Diameter of Binary Tree: traverse tree structure with correct state propagation and base cases. Pattern focus: DFS/BFS Traversal.

trees.name: Binary Tree Level Order Traversal
hints: A queue-based BFS naturally processes the tree level by level.
Track how many nodes are in the queue at the start of each level to know where each level ends.
trees.description: Binary Tree Level Order Traversal: traverse tree structure with correct state propagation and base cases. Pattern focus: DFS/BFS Traversal.

trees.name: Binary Tree Right Side View
hints: This is level-order traversal where you only keep the last node processed at each level.
During BFS, the last node dequeued at each level is the one visible from the right.
trees.description: Binary Tree Right Side View: traverse tree structure with correct state propagation and base cases. Pattern focus: DFS/BFS Traversal.

trees.name: Binary Tree Zigzag Level Order Traversal
hints: Same BFS level-order skeleton, but alternate the direction you read each level.
Track a boolean flag that flips each level, reversing the level's values when it says so.
trees.description: Binary Tree Zigzag Level Order Traversal: traverse tree structure with correct state propagation and base cases. Pattern focus: DFS/BFS Traversal.

trees.name: Lowest Common Ancestor of a Binary Search Tree
hints: A BST's ordering means you can decide which direction to go without checking both subtrees.
If both targets are less than the current node, go left; if both greater, go right; else you're at the LCA.
trees.description: Lowest Common Ancestor of a Binary Search Tree: traverse tree structure with correct state propagation and base cases. Pattern focus: LCA.

trees.name: Lowest Common Ancestor of a Binary Tree
hints: Without BST ordering, you generally need to search both subtrees and see where the targets 'diverge'.
If a node's left and right subtrees each return a non-null find, that node is the LCA.
trees.description: Lowest Common Ancestor of a Binary Tree: traverse tree structure with correct state propagation and base cases. Pattern focus: LCA.

trees.name: Validate Binary Search Tree
hints: A common mistake is only comparing a node to its immediate children — you need the full valid range.
Pass down a (min, max) valid range as you recurse; each node must fall within it, narrowing for children.
trees.description: Validate Binary Search Tree: traverse tree structure with correct state propagation and base cases. Pattern focus: BST validate/insert.

trees.name: Insert into a Binary Search Tree
hints: BST ordering tells you exactly which side to recurse into to find the insertion point.
If the value is less than the current node, insert into the left subtree (recursively); otherwise the right.
trees.description: Insert into a Binary Search Tree: traverse tree structure with correct state propagation and base cases. Pattern focus: Merge Technique.

trees.name: Kth Smallest Element in a BST
hints: An in-order traversal of a BST visits nodes in sorted order.
Do an in-order traversal and simply count until you reach the kth visited node.
trees.description: Kth Smallest Element in a BST: optimize for the best possible value while preserving problem constraints. Pattern focus: BST validate/insert.

trees.name: Construct Binary Tree from Preorder and Inorder Traversal
hints: The first element of preorder is always the root — find its position in inorder to split left/right.
Recursively build: root = preorder[0]; everything left of root in inorder is the left subtree, right is the right.
trees.description: Construct Binary Tree from Preorder and Inorder Traversal: traverse tree structure with correct state propagation and base cases. Pattern focus: Tree construction from traversals.

trees.name: Serialize and Deserialize Binary Tree
hints: Preorder traversal with explicit null markers lets you reconstruct the exact tree shape.
Serialize as 'val,left,right' recursively using a sentinel like '#' for null; deserialize consuming tokens in the same order.
trees.description: Serialize and Deserialize Binary Tree: traverse tree structure with correct state propagation and base cases. Pattern focus: Tree construction from traversals.

trees.name: Sliding Window Median
hints: This is the classic two-heaps pattern, but now with deletions as the window slides.
Keep a max-heap for the lower half and a min-heap for the upper half, and rebalance after every insert/remove.
trees.description: Sliding Window Median: maintain a moving contiguous range and keep it valid as boundaries shift. Pattern focus: Two Heaps.

trees.name: Lowest Common Ancestor of a Binary Search Tree
hints: A BST lets you decide whether to go left or right without searching both subtrees.
If both target values are smaller than the current node, go left; if both are larger, go right; otherwise you are at the LCA.
trees.description: Lowest Common Ancestor of a Binary Search Tree: traverse tree structure with correct state propagation and base cases. Pattern focus: LCA.

trees.name: Construct Binary Tree from Inorder and Postorder Traversal
hints: The last item in postorder is the root; find it in inorder to split left and right subtrees.
Recursively rebuild the right subtree first, then the left, because of how postorder is consumed from the end.
trees.description: Construct Binary Tree from Inorder and Postorder Traversal: traverse tree structure with correct state propagation and base cases. Pattern focus: Tree construction from traversals.

trees.name: Delete Node in a BST
hints: Deleting from a BST is mostly about preserving ordering after removing the target node.
Handle the three classic cases: leaf, one child, or two children by swapping with the in-order successor.
trees.description: Delete Node in a BST: traverse tree structure with correct state propagation and base cases. Pattern focus: BST validate/insert.

trees.name: Recover Binary Search Tree
hints: Two nodes in a BST were swapped by mistake; an inorder traversal exposes the two out-of-order values.
Track the previous node during inorder traversal, identify the two inversions, and swap the offending values back.
trees.description: Recover Binary Search Tree: traverse tree structure with correct state propagation and base cases. Pattern focus: BST validate/insert.

trees.name: Lowest Common Ancestor of Deepest Leaves
hints: Find the deepest leaves first, then work upward to the node that covers them all.
A postorder DFS that returns depth and candidate LCA together solves this elegantly.
trees.description: Lowest Common Ancestor of Deepest Leaves: identify constraints, choose an efficient strategy, and handle edge cases. Pattern focus: LCA.

trees.name: Construct Binary Tree from Preorder and Postorder Traversal
hints: The first preorder value is the root, and the next preorder value identifies the left subtree root.
Use the left subtree root to split postorder, then recursively rebuild both subtrees.
trees.description: Construct Binary Tree from Preorder and Postorder Traversal: traverse tree structure with correct state propagation and base cases. Pattern focus: Tree construction from traversals.

trees.name: Binary Tree Maximum Path Sum
hints: Return the best one-sided gain to parent, update global answer with both sides.
Negative child gains should be treated as zero.
trees.triggerQuestion: What phrase in the prompt reveals the intended pattern?
trees.pitfall: Write the brute force first, then name the repeated work or invariant before coding.
trees.description: Binary Tree Maximum Path Sum: optimize for the best possible value while preserving problem constraints. Pattern focus: Tree DP / Postorder.

trees.name: Vertical Order Traversal of a Binary Tree
hints: Assign each node (row, col), then sort by col, row, value.
DFS or BFS can collect coordinates; ordering decides correctness.
trees.triggerQuestion: What constraint makes the usual brute force too slow?
trees.pitfall: BFS order alone does not satisfy the tie-break by value.
trees.description: Vertical Order Traversal of a Binary Tree: traverse tree structure with correct state propagation and base cases. Pattern focus: Tree Coordinates / Ordered Traversal.

heaps.name: Last Stone Weight
hints: A max-heap lets you always grab the two heaviest stones efficiently.
Repeatedly pop the two largest, smash them, push the result back if nonzero, until at most one stone remains.
heaps.description: Last Stone Weight: identify constraints, choose an efficient strategy, and handle edge cases. Pattern focus: Top-K / Heap.

heaps.name: Kth Largest Element in an Array
hints: A min-heap of size k is a clean way to track the k largest elements seen so far.
Push elements onto the heap; whenever its size exceeds k, pop the smallest — the top is your answer.
heaps.description: Kth Largest Element in an Array: optimize for the best possible value while preserving problem constraints. Pattern focus: Top-K / Heap.

heaps.name: K Closest Points to Origin
hints: Same 'heap of size k' idea as Kth Largest, but ordered by distance from origin.
A max-heap of size k (by distance) lets you discard the farthest point whenever it grows past k.
heaps.description: K Closest Points to Origin: identify constraints, choose an efficient strategy, and handle edge cases. Pattern focus: Top-K / Heap.

heaps.name: Task Scheduler
hints: The most frequent task determines the minimum schedule length — think about spacing identical tasks n apart.
A max-heap by frequency, plus a cooldown queue, greedily schedules the most frequent available task each cycle.
heaps.description: Task Scheduler: identify constraints, choose an efficient strategy, and handle edge cases. Pattern focus: Top-K / Heap.

heaps.name: Reorganize String
hints: Similar spacing idea to Task Scheduler — no two adjacent characters can be the same.
Greedily place the most frequent remaining character using a max-heap, holding back the just-placed one for one round.
heaps.description: Reorganize String: enforce character-level constraints with efficient matching and state tracking. Pattern focus: Top-K / Heap.

heaps.name: Find Median from Data Stream
hints: Two heaps — a max-heap for the lower half, a min-heap for the upper half — find the median in O(log n) per insertion.
Keep the heaps balanced in size; the median is the top of the larger heap, or the average of both tops.
heaps.description: Find Median from Data Stream: identify constraints, choose an efficient strategy, and handle edge cases. Pattern focus: Two Heaps.

heaps.name: Car Pooling
hints: Think of pickups as +passengers and drop-offs as -passengers at points along a route.
Sort trips by start location, and use a min-heap of end times to know when passengers can be dropped.
heaps.description: Car Pooling: identify constraints, choose an efficient strategy, and handle edge cases. Pattern focus: Two Heaps.

heaps.name: Merge k Sorted Lists
hints: Merging two lists at a time is easy — the question is scaling to k lists efficiently.
A min-heap holding the current head of each list lets you always pull the smallest node in O(log k).
heaps.description: Merge k sorted linked lists efficiently into one sorted list.

heaps.name: Top K Frequent Words
hints: Count frequencies first, then use a heap or sorting rule that respects both frequency and lexicographic order.
For tie-breaking, compare words lexicographically after comparing counts.
heaps.description: Top K Frequent Words: identify constraints, choose an efficient strategy, and handle edge cases. Pattern focus: Top-K / Heap.

heaps.name: Smallest Range Covering Elements from K Lists
hints: You need the current min and max across k lists; a heap gives you the smallest current element efficiently.
Advance the list that contributed the current minimum and keep track of the best range seen so far.
heaps.description: Smallest Range Covering Elements from K Lists: optimize for the best possible value while preserving problem constraints. Pattern focus: Two Heaps.

graphs.name: Number of Islands
hints: Each unvisited land cell starts a new island — DFS or BFS from it to mark the whole island visited.
Flip visited land cells to water (or use a visited set); count how many times you start a fresh traversal.
graphs.description: Number of Islands: compute the total number of valid outcomes under the given constraints. Pattern focus: DFS/BFS Traversal.

graphs.name: Max Area of Island
hints: Same island-finding DFS/BFS as Number of Islands, but track and return the size of each island.
Have your DFS/BFS return the count of cells visited, tracking the maximum across all island starts.
graphs.description: Max Area of Island: optimize for the best possible value while preserving problem constraints. Pattern focus: DFS/BFS Traversal.

graphs.name: Clone Graph
hints: A hashmap from original node to cloned node prevents infinite loops in a cyclic graph.
DFS or BFS from the given node; before recursing into a neighbor, check if you've already cloned it.
graphs.description: Clone Graph: perform graph-style traversal and track visited state to avoid reprocessing. Pattern focus: DFS/BFS Traversal.

graphs.name: Pacific Atlantic Water Flow
hints: Instead of checking every cell against both oceans, work backwards — flood-fill inland FROM each ocean's border.
Run BFS/DFS from all Pacific-adjacent cells, and separately from Atlantic-adjacent cells; answer is reachable from both.
graphs.description: Pacific Atlantic Water Flow: identify constraints, choose an efficient strategy, and handle edge cases. Pattern focus: DFS/BFS Traversal.

graphs.name: Surrounded Regions
hints: Any 'O' connected to the border can never be surrounded — mark those first, then flip everything else.
DFS/BFS from every border 'O' to mark safe regions, flip remaining unmarked 'O's to 'X', unmark the safe ones back.
graphs.description: Surrounded Regions: identify constraints, choose an efficient strategy, and handle edge cases. Pattern focus: DFS/BFS Traversal.

graphs.name: Rotting Oranges
hints: This is multi-source BFS — start from ALL rotten oranges simultaneously.
Push all initially rotten oranges into the queue at once; each 'layer' processed is one minute passing.
graphs.description: Rotting Oranges: identify constraints, choose an efficient strategy, and handle edge cases. Pattern focus: DFS/BFS Traversal.

graphs.name: Redundant Connection
hints: As you add edges one by one, the first edge connecting two nodes already in the same component is redundant.
Union-Find: if two nodes are already unioned (same root) when you process their edge, that's your answer.
graphs.description: Redundant Connection: identify constraints, choose an efficient strategy, and handle edge cases. Pattern focus: Union-Find.

graphs.name: Number of Provinces
hints: Each connected component of cities is a province — Union-Find or DFS/BFS both find components directly.
Union every pair of directly-connected cities, then count how many distinct roots remain.
graphs.description: Number of Provinces: compute the total number of valid outcomes under the given constraints. Pattern focus: Union-Find.

graphs.name: Accounts Merge
hints: Accounts sharing any email belong to the same person — Union-Find on emails naturally groups them.
Union all emails within the same account together, group by final root, then merge account details.
graphs.description: Accounts Merge: compute the total number of valid outcomes under the given constraints. Pattern focus: Union-Find.

graphs.name: Satisfiability of Equality Equations
hints: Process all '==' equations first with Union-Find to build equivalence groups.
A '!=' equation fails only if both variables ended up in the same group after processing all '==' equations.
graphs.description: Satisfiability of Equality Equations: identify constraints, choose an efficient strategy, and handle edge cases. Pattern focus: Union-Find.

graphs.name: Course Schedule
hints: This is cycle detection in a directed graph — a cycle means courses can't all be completed.
Track node states (unvisited/visiting/visited) during DFS; hitting a 'visiting' node again means a cycle.
graphs.description: Course Schedule: identify constraints, choose an efficient strategy, and handle edge cases. Pattern focus: Topological Sort.

graphs.name: Course Schedule II
hints: Same cycle-detection idea, but you also need to output a valid ordering — this is topological sort.
Use DFS with a post-order stack, or Kahn's algorithm (BFS on indegree-0 nodes) to build the ordering.
graphs.description: Course Schedule II: identify constraints, choose an efficient strategy, and handle edge cases. Pattern focus: Topological Sort.

graphs.name: Minimum Height Trees
hints: Minimum-height trees have their root(s) near the tree's 'center' — repeatedly trimming leaves converges there.
Peel off leaf nodes layer by layer; the last 1-2 nodes remaining are the answer.
graphs.description: Minimum Height Trees: optimize for the best possible value while preserving problem constraints. Pattern focus: Topological Sort.

graphs.name: Network Delay Time
hints: This is a single-source shortest path problem — Dijkstra's algorithm is the standard tool.
Use a min-heap keyed by current shortest distance; the answer is the max shortest-distance, or -1 if unreachable.
graphs.description: Network Delay Time: identify constraints, choose an efficient strategy, and handle edge cases. Pattern focus: Dijkstra / Shortest Path.

graphs.name: Path With Minimum Effort
hints: Think of 'effort' as edge weight and minimize the maximum edge weight along a path — a Dijkstra variant.
Instead of summing weights, your 'distance' at each step is the max weight seen so far, still works with a priority queue.
graphs.description: Find a path from top-left to bottom-right that minimizes the maximum height difference on any step.

graphs.name: Cheapest Flights Within K Stops
hints: Standard Dijkstra doesn't directly respect a 'stop limit' — track stops used as part of your state.
Bellman-Ford relaxed k+1 times (or Dijkstra with (cost, node, stops) states) both handle the constraint correctly.
graphs.description: Cheapest Flights Within K Stops: identify constraints, choose an efficient strategy, and handle edge cases. Pattern focus: Dijkstra / Shortest Path.

graphs.name: Alien Dictionary
hints: Build precedence constraints between characters by comparing adjacent words.
Then perform a topological sort over the character graph; if you detect a cycle, the ordering is impossible.
graphs.description: Alien Dictionary: identify constraints, choose an efficient strategy, and handle edge cases. Pattern focus: Topological Sort.

graphs.name: Word Ladder
hints: Each word is a node; one-letter transformations are edges.
Precompute wildcard buckets like h*t to avoid comparing every pair.
graphs.triggerQuestion: What phrase in the prompt reveals the intended pattern?
graphs.pitfall: Write the brute force first, then name the repeated work or invariant before coding.
graphs.description: Word Ladder: identify constraints, choose an efficient strategy, and handle edge cases. Pattern focus: BFS Shortest Path.

graphs.name: Swim in Rising Water
hints: The path cost is the maximum elevation on the route, not sum of weights.
Use a min-heap frontier or binary-search time and test reachability.
graphs.triggerQuestion: What phrase in the prompt reveals the intended pattern?
graphs.pitfall: Write the brute force first, then name the repeated work or invariant before coding.
graphs.description: Swim in Rising Water: identify constraints, choose an efficient strategy, and handle edge cases. Pattern focus: Dijkstra / Binary Search on Answer.

graphs.name: Evaluate Division
hints: Each equation is a bidirectional weighted edge.
A query is path product from source to target.
graphs.triggerQuestion: What constraint makes the usual brute force too slow?
graphs.pitfall: Missing reverse edges makes half the queries impossible.
graphs.description: Evaluate Division: identify constraints, choose an efficient strategy, and handle edge cases. Pattern focus: Weighted Graph DFS.

graphs.name: Shortest Path in Binary Matrix
graphs.description: Return the length of the shortest clear path from the top-left cell to the bottom-right cell in a binary grid.
hints: Unweighted shortest path in a grid points to BFS, not DFS.
Explore all 8 directions and mark visited cells as soon as you enqueue them.

graphs.name: Time Needed to Inform All Employees
graphs.description: Compute how long it takes for a management tree to spread urgent news from the head to every employee.
hints: Convert the manager array into a children adjacency list.
Propagate accumulated time through the tree and take the maximum arrival time.

dp.name: Climbing Stairs
hints: The number of ways to reach step n is the sum of ways to reach step n-1 and n-2 — Fibonacci in disguise.
You only need the last two values at any point, so O(1) space works instead of a full array.
dp.description: Climbing Stairs: identify constraints, choose an efficient strategy, and handle edge cases. Pattern focus: 1D DP.

dp.name: House Robber
hints: At each house, either rob it (skip the previous) or skip it (keep the best so far).
dp[i] = max(dp[i-1], dp[i-2] + nums[i]); you only need the last two values.
dp.description: House Robber: define recurrence transitions and reuse solved subproblems. Pattern focus: 1D DP.

dp.name: House Robber II
hints: The houses are in a circle, so you can't rob both the first and last house — split into two cases.
Run the regular House Robber logic twice: once excluding the last house, once excluding the first, take the max.
dp.description: House Robber II: define recurrence transitions and reuse solved subproblems. Pattern focus: 1D DP.

dp.name: Decode Ways
hints: At each position, you can decode one digit alone, or sometimes two digits together — branches like Fibonacci.
dp[i] = dp[i-1] (if current digit valid alone) + dp[i-2] (if last two digits form a valid 10-26 code).
dp.description: Decode Ways: compute the total number of valid outcomes under the given constraints. Pattern focus: 1D DP.

dp.name: Coin Change
hints: For each amount up to the target, what's the fewest coins needed using previously computed smaller amounts?
dp[amount] = 1 + min(dp[amount - coin]) over all coins that fit; dp[0] = 0.
dp.description: Coin Change: identify constraints, choose an efficient strategy, and handle edge cases. Pattern focus: 1D DP.

dp.name: Word Break
hints: dp[i] = true if the string up to index i can be segmented into dictionary words.
For each i, check every possible last word ending at i — if dp[j] is true and s[j:i] is in the dictionary, dp[i] is true.
dp.description: Word Break: identify constraints, choose an efficient strategy, and handle edge cases. Pattern focus: 1D DP.

dp.name: Unique Paths
hints: The number of ways to reach any cell is the sum of ways to reach the cell above and the cell to the left.
dp[row][col] = dp[row-1][col] + dp[row][col-1]; first row/column each have one way.
dp.description: Unique Paths: perform graph-style traversal and track visited state to avoid reprocessing. Pattern focus: 2D DP / Knapsack.

dp.name: Unique Paths II
hints: Same recurrence as Unique Paths, but obstacle cells have zero ways to reach them.
Set dp value to 0 for any obstacle cell; otherwise sum from above and left as usual.
dp.description: Unique Paths II: perform graph-style traversal and track visited state to avoid reprocessing. Pattern focus: 2D DP / Knapsack.

dp.name: Unique Paths III
hints: This is a grid DFS/backtracking problem with a DP-ish state of visited cells rather than a plain count DP.
You need to start from the source, visit every non-obstacle cell exactly once, and count only paths that end at the target after covering them all.
dp.description: Unique Paths III: perform graph-style traversal and track visited state to avoid reprocessing. Pattern focus: DP on grids & trees.

dp.name: Minimum Path Sum
hints: Similar grid DP to Unique Paths, but minimizing a sum rather than counting paths.
dp[row][col] = grid[row][col] + min(dp[row-1][col], dp[row][col-1]).
dp.description: Minimum Path Sum: optimize for the best possible value while preserving problem constraints. Pattern focus: 2D DP / Knapsack.

dp.name: Partition Equal Subset Sum
hints: If the total sum is odd, it's immediately impossible — otherwise, can you find a subset summing to total/2?
Classic 0/1 knapsack — dp[s] = true if some subset sums to s; process each number once, high sums to low.
dp.description: Partition Equal Subset Sum: identify constraints, choose an efficient strategy, and handle edge cases. Pattern focus: 2D DP / Knapsack.

dp.name: Target Sum
hints: Assigning + or - to each number is equivalent to partitioning into two subsets with a specific difference.
Reduce to: find a subset summing to (totalSum + target) / 2, turning this into a knapsack-style count problem.
dp.description: Target Sum: identify constraints, choose an efficient strategy, and handle edge cases. Pattern focus: 2D DP / Knapsack.

dp.name: Coin Change II
hints: This time you're counting ways to make the amount, not minimum coins — order of selection matters here.
Iterate coins in the outer loop, amounts in the inner loop, so each combination is counted once.
dp.description: Coin Change II: identify constraints, choose an efficient strategy, and handle edge cases. Pattern focus: 2D DP / Knapsack.

dp.name: Longest Increasing Subsequence
hints: dp[i] represents the length of the longest increasing subsequence ending exactly at index i.
For O(n log n), maintain a 'tails' array and binary search for where each new element belongs.
dp.description: Longest Increasing Subsequence: optimize for the best possible value while preserving problem constraints. Pattern focus: LIS / LCS.

dp.name: Longest Common Subsequence
hints: Build a 2D table where dp[i][j] is the LCS length of the first i and first j characters of both strings.
If characters match, dp[i][j] = dp[i-1][j-1] + 1; otherwise dp[i][j] = max(dp[i-1][j], dp[i][j-1]).
dp.description: Longest Common Subsequence: optimize for the best possible value while preserving problem constraints. Pattern focus: LIS / LCS.

dp.name: Maximum Length of Repeated Subarray
hints: Similar 2D table to LCS, but the match must be a contiguous run, not just any subsequence.
dp[i][j] = dp[i-1][j-1] + 1 only if elements match; otherwise reset to 0.
dp.description: Maximum Length of Repeated Subarray: optimize for the best possible value while preserving problem constraints. Pattern focus: LIS / LCS.

dp.name: Edit Distance
hints: Three operations (insert, delete, replace) map naturally to three transitions in a 2D DP table.
If characters match, dp[i][j] = dp[i-1][j-1]; otherwise 1 + min(insert, delete, replace) from neighboring cells.
dp.description: Edit Distance: identify constraints, choose an efficient strategy, and handle edge cases. Pattern focus: LIS / LCS.

dp.name: House Robber III
hints: This is House Robber on a tree — for each node, decide whether robbing it is better, considering its children.
Have your recursive function return two values per node: max profit if robbed, and if not robbed.
dp.description: House Robber III: define recurrence transitions and reuse solved subproblems. Pattern focus: DP on grids & trees.

dp.name: Best Time to Buy and Sell Stock with Cooldown
hints: You need more than 'holding vs not holding' a stock — cooldown adds a third state after selling.
Track three states each day: holding, just sold (cooldown), free to buy — transition based on the day's price.
dp.description: Best Time to Buy and Sell Stock with Cooldown: optimize for the best possible value while preserving problem constraints. Pattern focus: DP on grids & trees.

dp.name: Minimum Falling Path Sum
hints: At each cell, the best path comes from one of the three cells above it.
Build the answer row by row with DP, updating each cell from the previous row's neighboring values.
dp.description: Minimum Falling Path Sum: optimize for the best possible value while preserving problem constraints. Pattern focus: DP on grids & trees.

dp.name: Regular Expression Matching
hints: Define dp[i][j] as whether s[i:] matches p[j:].
Star means zero of previous token or one-plus if current char matches.
dp.triggerQuestion: What phrase in the prompt reveals the intended pattern?
dp.pitfall: Write the brute force first, then name the repeated work or invariant before coding.
dp.description: Regular Expression Matching: identify constraints, choose an efficient strategy, and handle edge cases. Pattern focus: 2D DP / Recurrence Design.

dp.name: Burst Balloons
hints: Think backwards: choose which balloon is burst last inside an interval.
Padding with 1s at both ends makes boundary math cleaner.
dp.triggerQuestion: What phrase in the prompt reveals the intended pattern?
dp.pitfall: Write the brute force first, then name the repeated work or invariant before coding.
dp.description: Burst Balloons: identify constraints, choose an efficient strategy, and handle edge cases. Pattern focus: Interval DP.

dp.name: Distinct Subsequences
hints: If chars match, take ways using this char plus ways skipping it.
Use long-ish thinking for counts even when final type is number.
dp.triggerQuestion: What constraint makes the usual brute force too slow?
dp.pitfall: Confusing substring matching with subsequence counting changes the recurrence.
dp.description: Distinct Subsequences: define recurrence transitions and reuse solved subproblems. Pattern focus: 2D DP Counting.

dp.name: Delete and Earn
dp.description: Choose values to maximize points when taking one value deletes its adjacent numeric neighbors.
hints: Group equal numbers into total points per value first.
After compression, the choice becomes the same recurrence as House Robber on value buckets.

dp.name: Longest Arithmetic Subsequence
dp.description: Find the longest subsequence whose consecutive elements all share the same difference.
hints: A DP state needs both the current index and the chosen difference.
Store the best length ending at each index for each difference seen so far.

greedy.name: Merge Intervals
hints: Sort intervals by start time first — then overlapping intervals become adjacent.
Walk sorted intervals, merging the current one into the last result if they overlap, else starting new.
greedy.description: Merge Intervals: identify constraints, choose an efficient strategy, and handle edge cases. Pattern focus: Interval Merging.

greedy.name: Insert Interval
hints: Split into three zones: intervals fully before the new one, overlapping it, and fully after.
Merge all overlapping intervals into a single expanded interval before continuing to the ones after.
greedy.description: Insert Interval: identify constraints, choose an efficient strategy, and handle edge cases. Pattern focus: Interval Merging.

greedy.name: Non-overlapping Intervals
hints: Sort by end time — greedily keeping the interval that ends earliest leaves the most room for future ones.
Whenever the next interval overlaps the last kept one, remove it rather than the one you already kept.
greedy.description: Non-overlapping Intervals: identify constraints, choose an efficient strategy, and handle edge cases. Pattern focus: Interval Merging.

greedy.name: Interval List Intersections
hints: Two pointers, one per list, comparing overlaps and advancing whichever interval ends first.
The intersection of two overlapping intervals is [max(starts), min(ends)]; advance the earlier-ending pointer.
greedy.description: Interval List Intersections: identify constraints, choose an efficient strategy, and handle edge cases. Pattern focus: Interval Merging.

greedy.name: Jump Game
hints: Track the furthest index you can reach as you scan left to right.
At each reachable index i, update farthest = max(farthest, i + nums[i]); done if farthest reaches the end.
greedy.description: Jump Game: identify constraints, choose an efficient strategy, and handle edge cases. Pattern focus: Activity selection.

greedy.name: Jump Game II
hints: Think in 'levels' — like BFS, each jump reaches a new frontier of positions.
Track the current jump's boundary and the farthest reachable next jump; increment when you cross the boundary.
greedy.description: Jump Game II: identify constraints, choose an efficient strategy, and handle edge cases. Pattern focus: Activity selection.

greedy.name: Gas Station
